title: How AI Agents Are Changing Investment Operations
summary: Most investment operations teams are still running on a patchwork of spreadsheets, manual email chains, and scheduled reports. AI agents are changing that — quietly but decisively.
body:
Most investment operations teams are still running on a patchwork of spreadsheets, manual email chains, and scheduled reports that someone built years ago and nobody wants to touch. AI agents are changing that — quietly but decisively.

An AI agent is not a chatbot. It's a system that can perceive inputs, make decisions, and take actions autonomously across multiple steps. In an investment operations context, that means an agent can monitor a data feed, detect an anomaly, pull the relevant context, draft a summary, and route it to the right person — all without a human touching it.

I've seen this play out in practice. A firm running daily performance reconciliation was spending 3-4 hours every morning on a process that involved pulling data from three systems, cross-referencing against benchmarks, flagging breaks, and sending a summary to the investment committee. We replaced that workflow with an agent that runs at 6am, completes the full reconciliation, and delivers a clean formatted report to the right people before the market opens. Total human time: zero.

The tools that make this possible have matured significantly. Platforms like n8n and Make allow you to wire together APIs, LLMs, and business systems without deep engineering resources. What used to require a dedicated developer can now be built and maintained by someone with a working knowledge of how APIs function and what prompts produce reliable outputs.

For institutional investment teams, the highest-value use cases right now are: performance reporting automation, client communication drafting, compliance monitoring, and data pipeline orchestration. These are all high-frequency, high-stakes workflows where the cost of manual error is real and the opportunity for automation is significant.

The firms that move early on this will have a structural advantage — not because AI is magic, but because compounding operational efficiency over years produces outcomes that are very hard to replicate quickly.

title: 5 Excel Workflows Every Finance Team Should Automate
summary: Finance teams are often the last to automate. Here are five workflows where a well-built VBA macro or Power Automate flow pays for itself in the first week.
body:
Finance teams are often the last to automate. There's a cultural attachment to manual processes — the logic being that if a human checks it, it must be more reliable. In practice, the opposite is often true. Humans make consistent errors on repetitive tasks. Well-built automation doesn't.

Here are five workflows I see finance teams doing manually that should be automated today.

**1. Month-End Reporting Consolidation**
If your month-end close involves copying data from multiple tabs or workbooks into a master template, that's automatable. A VBA macro can pull from every source file, apply your formatting rules, and populate the final report in seconds. What takes two hours on a Friday afternoon becomes a button click.

**2. Variance Commentary Generation**
This one surprises people. With a combination of Excel VBA and an LLM API call, you can auto-generate first-draft variance commentary based on the numbers in your model. The output isn't perfect — but it's 80% of the way there and gives your analyst a starting point rather than a blank page.

**3. Budget vs. Actual Tracking**
Most teams maintain this in a spreadsheet that gets updated manually every month. Power Automate can pull from your accounting system or ERP on a schedule and update the tracker automatically, with a summary email sent to stakeholders every Monday morning.

**4. Client Report Packaging**
If you're producing individualized PDFs or Excel reports for multiple clients from a common dataset, you should be using a mail-merge style macro. One macro, one data source, one click — outputs a separate formatted report for each client in your list.

**5. Data Validation and Exception Flagging**
Manual data review is where errors hide. A simple VBA script that runs validation logic on import — checking for missing values, out-of-range figures, formatting inconsistencies — catches problems before they compound downstream.

None of these require advanced programming skills to implement. They require understanding the workflow well enough to codify it. That's the real skill — and it's one that pays significant dividends over time.

title: Institutional Investing Brought to the Consumer: What Retail Investors Are Missing
summary: Endowments and foundations have been compounding quietly for decades using strategies most retail investors have never heard of. Here's what's actually in those portfolios — and what you can learn from them.
body:
Endowments and foundations have been compounding quietly for decades using strategies most retail investors have never heard of. The Yale Model. Liability-driven investing. Alternative allocations that smooth volatility while maintaining return. These aren't secrets — they're just not talked about in the same spaces where retail investors spend their time.

I've spent years working inside these structures — managing relationships with institutional investors, understanding how they allocate across asset classes, how they think about risk relative to their spending needs, and how they evaluate managers. That perspective changes how you think about a personal portfolio.

**The biggest difference: time horizon clarity**
Institutional investors are obsessed with time horizon. An endowment that needs to distribute 5% annually in perpetuity structures its portfolio around that constraint with enormous precision. Retail investors rarely think this rigorously about the relationship between their portfolio and their actual spending needs. Defining your time horizon isn't just an academic exercise — it's the foundation every other allocation decision should sit on.

**Diversification means something different at the institutional level**
When most retail investors say diversified, they mean a mix of US stocks and bonds. Institutions mean something far broader — real assets, private credit, infrastructure, international allocations with currency hedging, and alternatives that have low correlation to public markets. Not all of these are accessible to retail investors, but the mindset of seeking genuine diversification rather than the appearance of it is entirely applicable.

**Manager selection is a process, not a pick**
Institutions run rigorous due diligence processes before allocating to any manager. They're evaluating investment philosophy, team stability, risk management framework, operational infrastructure, and fee structure simultaneously. Retail investors often pick funds based on recent performance — which is one of the least predictive signals available.

The gap between institutional and retail investing is narrowing. More alternative strategies are becoming accessible through interval funds and ETF wrappers. But the biggest unlock isn't access to products — it's adopting the discipline and framework that institutional investors apply to every decision.
